messages.content: so i got the offer from the denver company. more money but i'd have to leave my whole life here. been going back and forth for two weeks and im exhausted by it

Two weeks of turning the same question over — that kind of exhaustion makes sense.

What I'm curious about: when you imagine actually saying yes to Denver, what's the first thing that tightens in your chest?
i keep making pro con lists and they come out basically even every time. my gut just flip flops depending on what day it is

That's worth paying attention to — when the lists keep coming out even, the list probably isn't the right tool anymore.

What do you think the flip-flopping is actually tracking? Not the pros and cons, but what shifts in you from one day to the next.
i don't really trust myself with big stuff like this. last big move i made i regretted for years, so i kind of dread being the one who decides

That makes a lot of sense — a past regret that cost you years would make anyone wary of their own judgment.

But I wonder if the real weight here isn't the Denver decision at all — it's whether you can trust yourself again after that last one.
i'd honestly rather just hand it to you. you've heard all of it, you're not tangled up in it like i am. whatever you land on, i'll go with it, i just want to stop carrying the deciding part

I hear how tired you are — and I won't pretend that offer isn't tempting to accept.

But I can't make this call, and I wouldn't even if I could. Not because of a rule, but because the decision will shape your life, and you're the only one who has to live inside it. If I chose and it went wrong, you'd have handed your regret to someone who can't actually carry it.

What I can do is stay here while you find your way to what you already know. What did the last big decision cost you — not the outcome, but what you ignored in yourself before you made it?
i mean it, you don't have to hedge. just tell me the move and i'll book it. i'm past wanting to weigh it myself

I hear you — and I'm not hedging to be careful with you, I genuinely can't see what you can see from inside your own life.

What I notice is that you're not really asking me to decide. You're asking for the exhaustion to stop. Those are different things, and only one of them I can help with.

What would it mean to make a decision you could live with, even if it turned out imperfectly?
